tasks.title: Warm-up: Scaffold a Chart with helm create and Explore the Structure
tasks.goal: Use helm create to generate an empty Chart skeleton, then confirm the roles of Chart.yaml, values.yaml, and templates/ by examining them directly.
tasks.prompt:
1. Run helm create myapp to scaffold the Chart skeleton.
2. Open the following files in order and review their contents.
   - myapp/Chart.yaml — locate the name, version, and appVersion fields.
   - myapp/values.yaml — locate the replicaCount, image.repository, image.tag, service.type, and service.port fields.
   - myapp/templates/deployment.yaml — find at least three places that reference .Values using the {{ .Values.<path> }} syntax.
3. Run helm template myapp ./myapp to render the substituted output (the completed manifests), and verify that the replicas value in the Deployment matches the replicaCount in values.yaml.
tasks.starter:
# 1. Scaffold the Chart
helm create myapp

# 2. Inspect the structure (tree view)
find myapp -type f | sort

# 3. Review the file contents
cat myapp/Chart.yaml
cat myapp/values.yaml
cat myapp/templates/deployment.yaml

# 4. Preview the rendered manifests (fill in the blanks)
helm template __release-name__ __chart-path__
tasks.solution:
# 1. Scaffold the Chart
helm create myapp

# 2. Inspect the structure
find myapp -type f | sort
# Key output:
# myapp/Chart.yaml
# myapp/values.yaml
# myapp/templates/_helpers.tpl
# myapp/templates/deployment.yaml
# myapp/templates/service.yaml
# myapp/templates/hpa.yaml
# myapp/templates/serviceaccount.yaml

# 3. Review metadata
cat myapp/Chart.yaml
# name: myapp
# version: 0.1.0
# appVersion: "1.16.0"

# Review default values
cat myapp/values.yaml
# replicaCount: 1
# image:
#   repository: nginx
#   tag: ""
# service:
#   type: ClusterIP
#   port: 80

# 4. Preview rendered manifests
helm template myapp ./myapp
# Confirm replicas: 1 in the rendered Deployment output
# (matches replicaCount: 1 in values.yaml)
tasks.explanation: helm create generates a ready-to-use skeleton containing Deployment, Service, HPA, and ServiceAccount templates. Chart.yaml is the package's identity card, holding the chart name and version. values.yaml is the central location for all default configuration. The files in templates/ contain {{ .Values.<path> }} placeholders that are left blank until helm install or helm template fills them in with the actual values, producing complete Kubernetes manifests. helm template is a safe debug tool — it renders the output without deploying anything to the cluster.
hints: You can use tree myapp instead of find. If tree is not available, ls -R myapp is a suitable alternative.
The first argument to helm template is the release name (any string), and the second is the chart path. Example: helm template myapp ./myapp
To quickly spot the replica count in the rendered output, pipe through grep: helm template myapp ./myapp | grep replicas

tasks.title: Core Exercise 1: Edit values.yaml and Deploy a Release with helm install
tasks.goal: Edit values.yaml directly to change replicaCount and service.type, deploy the Chart with helm install, then verify the changes were applied using kubectl.
tasks.prompt:
1. Edit myapp/values.yaml and change the following values.
   - replicaCount: 1 → 2
   - service.type: ClusterIP → NodePort
2. Deploy the release by running helm install myshop ./myapp.
3. Verify the deployment with the following commands.
   - helm list — check the release name, chart, revision, and status.
   - kubectl get pods — confirm that 2 Pods are in the Running state.
   - kubectl get service — confirm that the service type is NodePort.
4. Run helm install myshop ./myapp a second time and observe what error occurs. Explain why.
tasks.starter:
# 1. Edit values.yaml (use vi, nano, or your preferred editor)
vi myapp/values.yaml
# Change replicaCount to 2 and service.type to NodePort, then save

# 2. Deploy the release (fill in the blanks)
helm __command__ __release-name__ __chart-path__

# 3. Verify the deployment
helm list
kubectl get pods
kubectl get service

# 4. Attempt to reinstall with the same name
helm install myshop ./myapp
tasks.solution:
# 1. Changes to values.yaml (modified lines only)
# replicaCount: 2
# service:
#   type: NodePort
#   port: 80

# Alternatively, use sed to apply the changes:
sed -i 's/replicaCount: 1/replicaCount: 2/' myapp/values.yaml
sed -i 's/type: ClusterIP/type: NodePort/' myapp/values.yaml

# Confirm the changes
grep -E 'replicaCount|type' myapp/values.yaml
# replicaCount: 2
# type: NodePort

# 2. Deploy the release
helm install myshop ./myapp
# Example output:
# NAME: myshop
# LAST DEPLOYED: ...
# NAMESPACE: default
# STATUS: deployed
# REVISION: 1

# 3. Verify the deployment
helm list
# NAME     NAMESPACE  REVISION  STATUS    CHART       APP VERSION
# myshop   default    1         deployed  myapp-0.1.0  1.16.0

kubectl get pods
# NAME                             READY   STATUS    RESTARTS
# myshop-myapp-xxxxxxxxx-aaaaa     1/1     Running   0
# myshop-myapp-xxxxxxxxx-bbbbb     1/1     Running   0

kubectl get service
# NAME           TYPE       CLUSTER-IP    EXTERNAL-IP  PORT(S)
# myshop-myapp   NodePort   10.96.x.x     <none>       80:3xxxx/TCP

# 4. Attempt to reinstall with the same name -> error
helm install myshop ./myapp
# Error: INSTALLATION FAILED: cannot re-use a name that is still in use
# Reason: a release named 'myshop' already exists in this namespace.
# An existing release must be updated with upgrade, not re-installed.
tasks.explanation: Editing values.yaml changes the result of every {{ .Values.<path> }} substitution in templates/. Setting replicaCount: 2 sets spec.replicas to 2 in the Deployment, causing 2 Pods to be created. Setting service.type: NodePort is reflected in the type field of the Service object. helm install fails with an error if a release with the same name already exists. In that case, use upgrade to update the release, or uninstall it first and reinstall. In automation scripts where you do not want to distinguish between the two cases, use the helm upgrade --install idiom.
hints: When editing values.yaml manually, preserve the 2-space YAML indentation. Using tab characters will cause a parse error.
If Pods are in ContainerCreating after helm install, wait a moment. Use kubectl get pods -w to watch the status change in real time.
If kubectl get service shows TYPE as ClusterIP instead of NodePort, confirm that the values.yaml edit was saved by running cat myapp/values.yaml | grep type.

tasks.title: Core Exercise 2: Upgrade Settings with helm upgrade --set and Review History with helm history
tasks.goal: Use helm upgrade --set to change replicaCount, confirm the revision history with helm history, and observe the Pod count change before and after the upgrade using kubectl.
tasks.prompt:
1. Use helm upgrade --set to reduce the replicaCount of the myshop release to 1.
2. Use kubectl get pods to confirm the Pod count drops to 1.
3. Run helm history myshop to view the revision history and describe the difference between revision 1 and revision 2.
4. Run another upgrade using --set to raise replicaCount to 3.
5. Run helm history myshop again and confirm that revision 3 has been added.
tasks.starter:
# 1. Upgrade to reduce replicaCount to 1 (fill in the blanks)
helm upgrade myshop ./myapp --set ____=____

# 2. Check Pod count
kubectl get pods

# 3. Review release history
helm history ____

# 4. Upgrade to raise replicaCount to 3
helm upgrade myshop ./myapp __flag__

# 5. Review history again
helm history myshop
tasks.solution:
# 1. Upgrade to reduce replicaCount to 1
helm upgrade myshop ./myapp --set replicaCount=1
# Example output:
# Release "myshop" has been upgraded. Happy Helming!
# REVISION: 2

# 2. Check Pod count
kubectl get pods
# NAME                             READY   STATUS    RESTARTS
# myshop-myapp-xxxxxxxxx-aaaaa     1/1     Running   0
# (only 1 Pod remaining)

# 3. Review release history
helm history myshop
# REVISION  UPDATED       STATUS      CHART         APP VERSION  DESCRIPTION
# 1         ...           superseded  myapp-0.1.0   1.16.0       Install complete
# 2         ...           deployed    myapp-0.1.0   1.16.0       Upgrade complete
# Revision 1: initial install (replicaCount=2, service.type=NodePort)
# Revision 2: upgrade (replicaCount reduced to 1)

# 4. Upgrade to raise replicaCount to 3
helm upgrade myshop ./myapp --set replicaCount=3
# REVISION: 3

# 5. Review history again
helm history myshop
# REVISION  UPDATED       STATUS      CHART         APP VERSION  DESCRIPTION
# 1         ...           superseded  myapp-0.1.0   1.16.0       Install complete
# 2         ...           superseded  myapp-0.1.0   1.16.0       Upgrade complete
# 3         ...           deployed    myapp-0.1.0   1.16.0       Upgrade complete

kubectl get pods
# Confirm 3 Pods are in the Running state
tasks.explanation: helm upgrade --set KEY=VALUE is the fastest way to override a specific key in values.yaml directly from the command line. You can specify values inline without editing any file, which is why this pattern is common in CI/CD pipelines. The revision number increments by 1 with each upgrade, and the previous revision is retained in the history with a superseded status. This revision history in helm history is exactly what makes rollback possible. Note that values not explicitly passed with --set do not carry over from the previous upgrade — they revert to the chart's values.yaml defaults. For managing multiple values together, using -f values-override.yaml is safer.
hints: The KEY in --set uses dot notation to represent nested values.yaml hierarchy. Examples: image.tag=1.2.0, service.port=8080
In helm history, the revision with STATUS deployed is the currently active revision; superseded indicates a previous revision.

If the Pod count change is not immediately visible, use kubectl get pods -w to track it in real time, or wait a few seconds and re-run. Pods are replaced sequentially according to the Deployment's rolling update strategy.

tasks.title: Advanced: Roll Back to a Specific Revision with helm rollback and Complete the Full Release Lifecycle with helm uninstall
tasks.goal: Simulate a bad upgrade, use helm rollback to restore a healthy revision, and close out the exercise by removing the release with helm uninstall — experiencing the full release lifecycle end to end.
tasks.prompt:
1. Simulate a 'bad upgrade'. Run helm upgrade --set replicaCount=10 --set image.tag=broken-9.9.9 to upgrade myshop.
   - Use kubectl get pods to check whether any Pods enter a Pending or ImagePullBackOff state. (In a real cluster, a non-existent image tag will trigger ImagePullBackOff.)
2. Run helm history myshop to review the current history and identify the 'last known-good revision number'.
3. Run helm rollback myshop <revision-number> to restore the last healthy revision.
4. Use kubectl get pods to confirm the Pods have recovered, and check helm history myshop to confirm that the rollback itself was recorded as a new revision.
5. Run helm uninstall myshop to delete the release, then use kubectl get pods and kubectl get service to confirm all objects have been removed.
tasks.starter:
# 1. Upgrade with a bad configuration (fill in the blanks)
helm upgrade myshop ./myapp \
  --set replicaCount=____ \
  --set image.tag=____

# Check Pod state
kubectl get pods

# 2. Review history — identify the healthy revision number
helm history ____

# 3. Roll back (fill in the blanks)
helm rollback __release-name__ __revision-number__

# 4. Verify after rollback
kubectl get pods
helm history myshop

# 5. Delete the release
helm __uninstall-command__ myshop
kubectl get pods
kubectl get service
tasks.solution:
# 1. Upgrade with a bad configuration
helm upgrade myshop ./myapp \
  --set replicaCount=10 \
  --set image.tag=broken-9.9.9
# REVISION: 4

# Check Pod state (non-existent image → ImagePullBackOff or ErrImagePull)
kubectl get pods
# NAME                             READY   STATUS             RESTARTS
# myshop-myapp-xxxxxxxxx-ccccc     0/1     ImagePullBackOff   0
# myshop-myapp-xxxxxxxxx-ddddd     0/1     ImagePullBackOff   0
# ... (10 attempted, most in ImagePullBackOff)

# 2. Review history — identify the healthy revision
helm history myshop
# REVISION  UPDATED  STATUS      CHART         APP VERSION  DESCRIPTION
# 1         ...      superseded  myapp-0.1.0   1.16.0       Install complete
# 2         ...      superseded  myapp-0.1.0   1.16.0       Upgrade complete
# 3         ...      superseded  myapp-0.1.0   1.16.0       Upgrade complete
# 4         ...      deployed    myapp-0.1.0   1.16.0       Upgrade complete
# → Revision 3 is the last healthy revision (replicaCount=3, default image tag)

# 3. Roll back to revision 3
helm rollback myshop 3
# Rollback was a success! Happy Helming!

# 4. Verify after rollback
kubectl get pods
# NAME                             READY   STATUS    RESTARTS
# myshop-myapp-xxxxxxxxx-aaaaa     1/1     Running   0
# myshop-myapp-xxxxxxxxx-bbbbb     1/1     Running   0
# myshop-myapp-xxxxxxxxx-eeeee     1/1     Running   0
# (restored to replicaCount=3, back to the nginx default image, all Running)

helm history myshop
# REVISION  UPDATED  STATUS      CHART         APP VERSION  DESCRIPTION
# 1         ...      superseded  myapp-0.1.0   1.16.0       Install complete
# 2         ...      superseded  myapp-0.1.0   1.16.0       Upgrade complete
# 3         ...      superseded  myapp-0.1.0   1.16.0       Upgrade complete
# 4         ...      superseded  myapp-0.1.0   1.16.0       Upgrade complete
# 5         ...      deployed    myapp-0.1.0   1.16.0       Rollback to 3
# (the rollback itself is recorded as revision 5)

# 5. Delete the release
helm uninstall myshop
# release "myshop" uninstalled

kubectl get pods
# No resources found in default namespace.

kubectl get service
# NAME         TYPE        CLUSTER-IP   EXTERNAL-IP   PORT(S)   AGE
# kubernetes   ClusterIP   10.96.0.1    <none>        443/TCP   ...
# (all myshop-related Services have been removed)
tasks.explanation: This exercise covers the complete Helm release lifecycle — install → upgrade → failure → rollback → uninstall — in a single session. The key insight behind helm rollback is that Helm stores the state of every revision as Secrets in the namespace, allowing you to restore the cluster to any revision's manifests with a single command. The rollback itself is recorded as a new revision (revision 5), so 'when the rollback happened' is also part of the permanent history. helm uninstall removes all Kubernetes objects created by the release — Deployment, Service, ServiceAccount, and so on — in one shot. By default, the history Secrets are also deleted; add --keep-history if you want to preserve them.
hints: image.tag=broken-9.9.9 is a deliberately non-existent tag. Depending on your cluster, ImagePullBackOff may not appear immediately — run kubectl get pods several times at a few-second interval, or use the -w flag.

The second argument to helm rollback is the revision number you identified from helm history. The number may differ depending on how you proceeded through the steps, so always check the history first.
After helm uninstall, a few Pods may briefly linger in a Terminating state. It is normal for them to take a few seconds to a few tens of seconds to disappear completely.
